title: Plumbing, Heating, and Air-Conditioning Contractors

narrative.executive_view: The selected residential and light-commercial service/replacement contractor remains a paid, accountable physical operator in an AI-mature market. AI is useful primarily in its office, selling, scheduling, documentation, and estimating workflows; the wage-heavy installation, repair, testing, and refrigerant-handling work remains on site. The initial economics are therefore conditional rather than a labor-elimination thesis, and the broad-code target count and owner supply require evidence before underwriting. [S1][S2][S3]
narrative.target_archetype: The target is a multi-location or scaled local residential and light-commercial HVAC/plumbing service and replacement contractor with employed field technicians, a dispatch/CSR office, and some maintenance-agreement revenue. It excludes new-construction mechanical subcontractors, industrial pipefitting/plant work, pure plumbing-only shops, and one-person owner-operator businesses. This is the operational model in which a buyer can centralize administrative AI tools while retaining accountable licensed field work, but NAICS 238220 does not publish a size-band split by this model. [S2]
narrative.how_ai_changes_work: AI can compress intake, call summaries, scheduling, dispatch support, job documentation, price-book preparation, marketing, and portions of estimating and supervision. It does not itself install pipe, diagnose and repair equipment in a building, move materials, test systems, or handle refrigerant. The role ranges deliberately assign only small task shares to plumbers, HVAC technicians, sheet-metal workers, and helpers despite AI-enabled access to manuals and documentation. [S1][S2][S3][S4]
narrative.value_capture: The selected service/replacement model commonly supports upfront flat-rate price books and may sell maintenance agreements, which are more compatible with retaining a portion of back-office savings than audited hourly cost-plus work. That is only a pricing-structure proxy, not a measurement of twenty-four-month savings retention: price-book competition, lead platforms, vendor charges, and the mix of commercial bid work can pass savings through. The retention range is consequently broad. [S2][S5]
narrative.adoption_timing: The closest current survey covers commercial and residential contractors across HVAC, plumbing, and other trades rather than this EBITDA band. It reports 12% with AI embedded in operations and 34% experimenting, with administrative work leading current use. That supports a positive but sub-50% starting point; the stated time to 50% is an extrapolation and is not evidence that field work is autonomously performed. [S4]
narrative.buyability: The deterministic NAICS estimate supplies 1,152 firms in the $1–10M EBITDA band, but the selected service/replacement archetype is not the entire NAICS code. Current HVAC M&A activity and named residential platforms show meaningful buyer competition, while they do not demonstrate how many 238220 band firms fit this archetype or establish excess succession supply. The record therefore uses the preregistered missing-coverage and missing-seller defaults rather than relabeling all 1,152 firms as targets. [S6][S7]
narrative.valuation: Lower-middle-market home-services valuation evidence is dispersed and advisory-produced. The available 2024–early-2026 proxy places HVAC and plumbing outcomes in broad, overlapping bands, while a sector-level report reports materially higher averages that can include larger and commercial assets. Entry and exit are set to the same broad current-market reference range rather than asserting multiple expansion from AI or an unobserved roll-up premium. [S6][S8]
narrative.operator_survival: Operator survival is durable, with pressure as a plausible downside: the acquired firm still sends people to homes and buildings to install, inspect, repair, and test systems. BLS describes those on-site duties and EPA requires certification for technicians who service equipment that can release refrigerant. These rules and physical responsibilities preserve paid work but do not guarantee that a local contractor retains every customer relationship or all margin as national platforms and OEMs improve digital channels. [S1][S2][S3]
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The greatest uncertainty is scope, not a precision problem: 238220 blends residential service, commercial service, new construction, pipefitting, and mechanical contracting, and the supplied band construction likely omits many smaller but potentially eligible high-margin service businesses. There is no target-archetype coverage observation or owner-succession measurement. Labor share is also ancestor-level and low-quality. AI vendors' surveys may overrepresent software-engaged contractors, while transaction ranges are not a completed-deal dataset for this exact target.

sources.title: BLS Occupational Outlook Handbook: Plumbers, Pipefitters, and Steamfitters
sources.what_it_supports: Physical installation, repair, testing, tools, licensing, and apprenticeship characteristics used to bound plumber/pipefitter task exposure and survival.

sources.title: BLS Occupational Outlook Handbook: Heating, Air Conditioning, and Refrigeration Mechanics and Installers
sources.what_it_supports: On-site HVAC installation, maintenance, repair, inspection, service-contract, contractor-employer, and regulatory-duty evidence for the named archetype.

sources.title: EPA Section 608 Technician Certification
sources.what_it_supports: Federal certification requirement for technicians maintaining, servicing, repairing, or disposing of equipment that could release covered refrigerants.

sources.title: ServiceTitan 2026 State of AI in the Trades
sources.what_it_supports: Survey proxy for current contractor AI embedding, experimentation, administrative use, efficiency outcomes, and adoption barriers across HVAC, plumbing, and adjacent trades.

sources.title: Housecall Pro HVAC Flat Rate Pricing Software
sources.what_it_supports: Industry-vendor evidence that HVAC service contractors use upfront, competitive flat-rate price books that incorporate labor, overhead, and materials.

sources.title: Capstone Partners: HVAC Services M&A Update
sources.what_it_supports: Current HVAC-services deal activity, financial-sponsor add-on activity, fragmentation, and broader sector valuation context; not a count of exact targets.

sources.title: Redwood Services acquisition of Sierra Platform
sources.what_it_supports: A current named example of an active residential HVAC/plumbing consolidator, a multi-company platform, and maintenance-agreement density.

sources.title: CT Acquisitions: 2026 Home Services M&A Multiples Report
sources.what_it_supports: Low-quality advisory proxy for 2024–early-2026 lower-middle-market HVAC and plumbing adjusted-EBITDA multiple ranges and size/mix dispersion.

labor_share.method: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 23822 ($100,763,014,046) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 23822 ($306,277,104,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: ancestor 23822. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

occupations.title: Heating, Air Conditioning, and Refrigeration Mechanics and Installers
First-Line Supervisors of Construction Trades and Extraction Workers
First-Line Supervisors of Mechanics, Installers, and Repairers
Sales Representatives of Services, Except Advertising, Insurance, Financial Services, and Travel

n_band.derivation: margin 0.0796 (margins.json[23]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Engineering/Construction EBITDA/Sales 7.96%); revenue/employee $260,272 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 238220); class <5: 63,588 firms, avg revenue/firm $386,166 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $30,739 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 20,175 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,348,756 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $107,361 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 12,459 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,960,335 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $235,643 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 9,415 firms, avg revenue/firm $9,892,743 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $787,462 -> out of $1-10M band; class 100-499: 1,152 firms, avg revenue/firm $49,865,618 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $3,969,303 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 215 firms, avg revenue/firm $311,824,335 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $24,821,217 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 1,152

ai_replaceable_share.rationale: BLS and EPA establish that the dominant trade roles perform physical, accountable, often licensed work; the trade survey indicates the early AI use is concentrated in administration. Fractions are bounded task-compression judgments, not claims that AI replaces an occupation.
caveats: The supplied OEWS mix is at 23822 rather than the named archetype and leaves a material residual wage share.
No source measures task fractions for this target EBITDA band; all within-role fractions are conservative estimates.
AI-assisted diagnostics or documentation may improve field productivity without making a field role remotely substitutable.

breakdown_by_role.rationale: Installation, repair, testing, tools, code work, and on-site troubleshooting dominate; documentation and limited estimating support are the bounded AI-addressable tasks. [S1]

breakdown_by_role.role: Heating, Air Conditioning, and Refrigeration Mechanics and Installers
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Technicians physically install, maintain, inspect, repair, and replace HVACR systems; manuals, notes, and diagnostics are the small AI-addressable share. [S2][S3]

breakdown_by_role.role: First-Line Supervisors of Construction Trades and Extraction Workers
breakdown_by_role.rationale: On-site crew coordination and quality control persist, while schedules, progress reporting, and job-costing support can compress. [S1][S4]
General clerical work is the closest listed office proxy for intake, records, invoicing, and communications, where the survey reports early AI concentration. [S4]
Sheet-metal fabrication and installation remain physical; only documentation, layout assistance, and limited takeoff support are included. [S2]
Operations managers retain customer, staffing, exception, and accountability decisions, while reporting, planning, and analysis can compress. [S4]
Helpers move materials and support installation physically; digital training and documentation are the only included slice. [S1]

breakdown_by_role.role: First-Line Supervisors of Mechanics, Installers, and Repairers
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Field supervision remains accountable for work quality and safety, with partial scheduling, reporting, and performance-analysis compression. [S2][S4]
Installation and repair helpers are physical support labor; the small allowance is for task instructions and records. [S2]

breakdown_by_role.role: Sales Representatives of Services, Except Advertising, Insurance, Financial Services, and Travel
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Lead qualification, follow-up, proposal drafting, and financing paperwork can compress, but in-home consultation and close responsibility persist. [S4]
The residual plausibly contains dispatch, CSR, bookkeeping, estimators/project managers, and additional field roles. It is given a middle range rather than treated as all office work. [S1][S2][S4]

target_archetype.name: Residential and light-commercial HVAC/plumbing service and replacement contractor
inclusion_criteria: US contractor primarily serving residential and light-commercial repair, maintenance, replacement, and small retrofit customers.
Employed field technicians with a dispatch/CSR and administrative office, and capacity to use shared operating systems.
Meaningful HVAC and/or plumbing service work, with maintenance agreements or recurring service as a relevant but not required feature.
Plausible $1–10M EBITDA target, subject to transaction-specific normalization.
exclusion_criteria: Ground-up new-construction mechanical subcontractors whose economics are primarily GC bid and project based.
Industrial pipefitting, process piping, fire-sprinkler, or heavy commercial mechanical specialists.
Pure plumbing-only shops, one-person owner-operator businesses, distributors, OEMs, and lead-generation platforms.
Electrical contracting work classified outside NAICS 238220.
target_archetype.selection_basis: This is the most investable service-oriented model within the broad code because it combines recurring/replacement demand, a centralized office workflow for AI deployment, and field work that retains accountable local delivery. BLS confirms that HVAC technicians commonly work for 238220 contractors and that residential technicians may sell service contracts, but it does not establish the archetype’s share of the supplied size band. [S2]

coverage.rationale: No published source found splits the supplied 1,152-firm EBITDA-band estimate by residential/light-commercial service and replacement versus new construction, commercial mechanical, pipefitting, or other 238220 models.
caveats: The finalizer must use its preregistered missing-coverage point and 0–1 sensitivity rather than treating the full NAICS band as this archetype.
The supplied n_band relies on a construction-margin proxy and may omit smaller high-margin service/replacement businesses.

retained_savings_share_24m.rationale: Upfront flat-rate price books and service contracts provide a commercial channel for retaining a portion of administrative savings, unlike automatic hourly cost reimbursement. The midpoint allows for software cost, competitive price-book resetting, lead costs, and commercial bid-work pass-through.
caveats: No source directly measures net savings retention after 24 months for this target archetype.
Residential flat-rate and maintenance-agreement behavior may not represent commercial or construction revenue in the selected operator.
The range does not double count mature operator survival; it covers only near-term retention of gross operating savings.

t50_years.rationale: A 2025 survey of 1,032 residential and commercial contractors across seven trades reports 12% embedded AI use and 34% active experimentation, with administrative tasks leading. Four years is a disclosed extrapolation for 50% material use in the target’s AI-addressable workflows, not an observation.
caveats: The survey is not a census and does not isolate $1–10M EBITDA HVAC/plumbing service/replacement contractors.
Experimentation is not counted as effective material adoption.
The estimate concerns office, sales, and supervision tasks represented in V, not autonomous field installation or repair.

current_adoption_pct.rationale: The 12% embedded-AI result is used as the closest broad-trades proxy for current material adoption; the 34% experimenting figure is deliberately not treated as embedded adoption.
caveats: The survey spans seven trades and mixed company sizes, so it is not direct evidence for the selected target archetype.
This contextual input does not itself determine the adoption factor; t50 does.

historical_analogs.value: Embedded field-service software adoption is the closest operational analogy: current AI use is concentrated in administration, marketing, scheduling, dispatch, and workflow support rather than replacement of on-site field labor.
historical_analogs.rationale: The survey’s observed department pattern supplies a proximate adoption analogy for the target’s office workflows.
caveats: It is not a completed historical adoption curve and cannot validate the t50 range.

seller_supply_signal.rationale: No current, target-archetype-specific owner-age, succession, listing, or sale-rate evidence was found that would justify excess, normal, or scarce seller supply.
caveats: Unknown owner demographics are not converted into a negative seller-supply adjustment.

active_consolidators.rationale: Current HVAC-services M&A evidence reports 92 announced or completed 2026 transactions and 38 PE add-ons, while Redwood’s current residential HVAC/plumbing platform example demonstrates direct archetype overlap. Eight is a conservative count of buyer platforms plausibly active for this type of service target, with a deliberately wide range rather than a claim that all sector buyers bid for every target.
caveats: The sources do not publish a deduplicated national buyer count for this exact EBITDA band and archetype.
Buyer activity varies substantially by region, service mix, growth, and maintenance-agreement density.

buy_mult.rationale: The advisory report’s lower-middle-market HVAC and plumbing matrices provide broad adjusted-EBITDA ranges that overlap the selected service/replacement operator. Six times is a blended current-market reference, not a company valuation or a completed-deal observation for this exact target.
caveats: The source is an advisory report, not a public transaction database, and mixes EBITDA bands below the target’s lower bound with the rest of the lower middle market.
Owner compensation, maintenance-agreement density, commercial mix, geography, customer concentration, and process quality can move a target materially within or outside the proxy range.

exit_mult.rationale: The same broad current-market range is used for exit because the available evidence does not establish a defensible AI-driven or roll-up-driven spread. A flat base is intentionally neutral under the frozen instrument.
caveats: The source does not observe the future exit market for an acquired and integrated target.
The low/high bounds express transaction dispersion, not a forecast that the buyer will capture expansion.

pricing_structure.value: Residential service and replacement can use upfront flat-rate price books and maintenance agreements; commercial and new-construction work may be more bid- or contract-driven.
pricing_structure.rationale: Industry-vendor price-book material and BLS’s residential HVAC service-contract reference support the selected model’s pricing proxy.
caveats: The actual mix must be measured in diligence; it materially affects 24-month retention of AI savings.

operator_survival.rationale: The acquired operator remains responsible for physical installation, inspection, testing, repair, and, for covered equipment, certified refrigerant work. That supports durable paid demand for the operator rather than merely durable demand for the underlying service.
caveats: DURABLE does not mean margin is protected: national platforms, OEM channels, remote monitoring, and lead intermediaries can pressure price or control the customer relationship.
PRESSURED is included as the downside plausible class for those commercial and channel risks, separate from the 24-month savings-retention estimate.

cross_checks.uplift_vs_timing_consistent: Current embedded adoption is below 50% in the closest contractor survey, so t50 is positive. The four-year base and two-to-seven-year range describe adoption of office, sales, and supervision workflows; they do not imply replacement of the physical field roles that constrain V. [S4]
cross_checks.capture_vs_survival_separated: Capture estimates the share of gross savings retained after roughly 24 months under price-book and contract competition. Survival separately asks whether the operator remains paid at mature AI adoption; physical on-site and certified work support DURABLE, while platform/OEM pressure remains a separate downside class. [S1][S2][S3][S5]
cross_checks.no_deal_evidence_in_VA: No M&A, valuation, consolidation, ownership, pricing, or adoption evidence was used to raise any role fraction. V role judgments use only job-task, physical-work, licensing, and administrative-use evidence. [S1][S2][S3][S4]
cross_checks.multiple_neutrality_acknowledged: The same base current-market multiple is used at entry and exit because available proxy evidence does not establish a defensible spread. Flat multiples are neutral by the frozen v4 method; the operating-value thesis is not duplicated in M. [S8]

dataset_mismatch.description: The supplied labor share is ancestor-level 23822, uses 2024 wages against 2022 receipts, and is marked LOW quality. The supplied n_band uses an Engineering/Construction margin proxy and places only the 100–499 employee class in band; this may understate eligible residential service/replacement businesses in smaller size classes with higher margins.
sensitivity: V is mechanically sensitive to the low-quality ancestor labor-share input.
B uses the supplied n_band but does not treat all 1,152 firms as target-archetype firms because coverage is MISSING.
Actual target EBITDA normalization should separate owner compensation, service/replacement mix, and new-construction cyclicality.

subsegments: Industrial pipefitting and related specialty work
market_boundary.selection_basis: The selected service/replacement archetype has centralized office workflows, customer-service opportunities, and continuing accountable on-site work. It is not asserted to dominate the entire NAICS code, and its unknown share of n_band is explicitly scored as MISSING. [S2]

top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: Determines effective target density and makes readiness UNPROVEN; it prevents treating all 1,152 broad-code firms as service/replacement targets.
No succession evidence supports an excess or scarcity adjustment, so buyability cannot be treated as fully evidenced.

top_uncertain_inputs.range: Weighted within-role fraction 0.082635–0.249280 before the deterministic labor-share multiplication
top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: The residual wage mix and task-compression assumptions determine a small physical-trades V and its wide sensitivity.
Local price-book competition, service-agreement density, commercial bid work, and software/lead costs determine whether office savings remain margin.
Transaction dispersion produces a wide M sensitivity; the flat base intentionally does not assume expansion.

reviewer_note: Research packet only. The broad-code target-archetype coverage and seller-supply evidence remain MISSING by design; the finalizer must preserve those missing states and their preregistered neutral/wide sensitivity defaults rather than converting them into measured negative economics.
